frame.text: La carte est morte — aucun rail ne monte, aucune LED. Fais le diagnostic et donne-moi un protocole de mesure pour trouver la panne.

I've got everything I need. Here's my reasoning, then I'll show you the chain on the board and propose a guided protocol.

## Analysis

The symptom "no rail, no LED" tells you something valuable: **even the *always-on* domain is dead**. On the Reform, as soon as 24 V comes in, a *standby* stage (U14) permanently produces the **LPC_VCC** rail that wakes the system microcontroller (U18). If *nothing* lights up — neither the input LED D9 nor the LPC LED D11 — then the problem is right at the **front**, before the sequencer even gets a say.

The input chain is: **J1 (24 V jack) → F1 (fuse) → VIN (test at R49) → U14 (standby regulator) → LPC_VCC**.

**1. Suspect #1: `F1`, the SMD input fuse (~0.50).** It's the most common cause of a completely dead Reform (rule `rule-vin-dead-001`, confidence 0.80): it blows on an overcurrent or a reverse polarity and cuts everything downstream. Behind it: a **short on VIN** (~0.25) pulling the supply into current limit, and the jack **J1** (~0.10, cracked joint).

**2. Discriminating measurement.** A single measurement cuts the problem in half: **the 24 V at test point R49** (just after the fuse).
- If you read ~24 V at R49 → F1 and the input are healthy, the fault is downstream (U14/standby).
- If you read ~0 V at R49 but ~24 V at F1's input → **F1 is open**.

⚠️ **Before replacing F1, we look for a short**: a fuse doesn't blow on its own. If VIN is shorted to ground, a fresh fuse will blow again immediately. So we run the "board off" tests (ohmmeter/diode) **first**.

**3. Fallback plan.** If F1 is good and VIN reaches R49 fine but the board stays dead → we move down to **U14** (3.3 V standby regulator): 0 V on its LPC_VCC output = U14 dead or a short on the rail.

One important note about your toolkit: you have **no hot air and no bench supply**. No problem — the whole diagnostic runs on the multimeter, and if we have to chase a short, your **thermal camera** will be our best friend (power it up, see which part heats). We'll replace F1 with an iron, not hot air.

I'll show you the chain and propose the protocol 👇

input.rationale: Symptom "no rail, no LED" = always-on domain dead → fault upstream of everything (rule-vin-dead-001, conf. 0.80). Start with the board-off tests (zero risk) to confirm F1 and rule out a short on VIN, BEFORE replacing anything, then power up to locate where the 24 V drops and check the U14 standby.

steps.instruction: Board OFF (nothing connected). Multimeter in ohmmeter mode, one probe on each F1 pad.
steps.rationale: Tests the fuse continuity directly. OL = blown fuse, our suspect #1.

steps.instruction: Board OFF. Diode mode, black probe on GND, red probe on the VIN side of R49. Note the value.
steps.rationale: Detects a VIN→GND short BEFORE replacing F1: a short would blow a fresh fuse again. <0.05 V = hard short to chase.

steps.instruction: Plug in the 24 V adapter (batteries removed). V DC mode, measure 24V_IN on F1 / J1's input pad.
steps.rationale: Confirms the wall supply actually reaches the board. 0 V here = adapter, cable or jack J1 at fault, not the board.

steps.instruction: Still powered. V DC mode, measure VIN at R49 (after the fuse).
steps.rationale: 24 V at J1 but 0 V here ⇒ F1 is open. This is the measurement that separates F1 from everything else.

steps.instruction: Powered. V DC mode, measure U14's LPC_VCC output (the 3V3 standby output pin).
steps.rationale: If VIN arrives fine but the board stays dead: 0 V here = U14 dead or a short on LPC_VCC. Separates the standby stage from the input.

steps.instruction: Summary: we target F1 (iron replacement), a short on VIN (component hunt with the thermal camera), or U14 depending on which point drops.
steps.rationale: Steers the next step toward the right repair based on the results.

input.title: Dead board — hunting the VIN input rail

input.content:
# Repair 7a2ae4c8feaf — MNT Reform motherboard

## Symptom
"Aucun rail, carte morte" — no rails, no LEDs at all (D9 power-input + D11 LPC_VCC both dark per discussion). Always-on standby domain is dead → fault upstream of the sequencer.

## Topology (input chain)
J1 (jack 24V, net 24V_IN) → F1 (SMD input fuse) → VIN (test point R49) → U14 (always-on 3V3 LDO → LPC_VCC) → U18 (LPC MCU sequencer).
Main converters downstream: U7 (+5V), U12 (+3V3), enabled by U18.

## Top suspects (rule-vin-dead-001, conf 0.80)
1. F1 open (blown input fuse) ~0.50 — most common dead-Reform cause.
2. Short on VIN to GND ~0.25 — would re-blow a fresh fuse; MUST clear before replacing F1.
3. J1 barrel jack cold joint ~0.10.
Fallback branch: U14 dead / LPC_VCC short (rule-lpc-vcc-dead-002, conf 0.78) if VIN present but board dead.

## Tech constraints (beginner)
Tools: multimeter, scope, thermal_camera, soldering_iron, microscope, reballing_kit, uv_lamp, stencil_printer.
NO hot air, NO bench PSU, NO preheater. → short-hunt via thermal cam (powered) + diode-mode; F1 swap at iron not hot air.
short_isolation skill = unlearned → explain step by step.

## Plan / status
Proposed guided protocol (6 steps): cold F1 continuity → cold VIN→GND diode short test → powered 24V@J1 → powered VIN@R49 → powered LPC_VCC@U14 → ack/branch.
STATUS: protocol proposed, awaiting tech's first measurements.

Board OFF. Diode mode, black probe on GND, red probe on the VIN side of R49. Note the value.
Plug in the 24 V adapter (batteries removed). V DC mode, measure 24V_IN on F1 / J1's input pad.
Still powered. V DC mode, measure VIN at R49 (after the fuse).
Powered. V DC mode, measure U14's LPC_VCC output (the 3V3 standby output pin).
Summary: we target F1 (iron replacement), a short on VIN (component hunt with the thermal camera), or U14 depending on which point drops.

frame.text:
The protocol is laid on the board (6 steps, numbered badges) and the input chain is highlighted: **J1 → F1 → R49(VIN) → U14**, with the two indicator LEDs (D9, D11) in grey.

We start **cold, nothing plugged in** — zero risk:

**Step 1 — F1 continuity.** Multimeter in ohmmeter mode, one probe on each leg of `F1` (the small 2-pin part right after the jack).
- **0–1 Ω** → fuse good, we move on to the possible short.
- **OL / open circuit** → blown fuse, our suspect #1 is confirmed… but we do **not** replace it yet: step 2 checks there's no short that blew it.

Give me the value you read (you can type it here, like "F1 = OL" or "0.3 Ω"), and I'll take you to the next step. If a step asks for a tool or a point you can't find, tell me and we'll adapt.

steps.instruction: Board OFF (nothing connected). Multimeter in ohmmeter mode, one probe on each F1 pad.
steps.rationale: Tests the fuse continuity directly. OL = blown fuse, our suspect #1.

steps.instruction: Board OFF. Diode mode, black probe on GND, red probe on the VIN side of R49. Note the value.
steps.rationale: Detects a VIN→GND short BEFORE replacing F1: a short would blow a fresh fuse again. <0.05 V = hard short to chase.

steps.instruction: Plug in the 24 V adapter (batteries removed). V DC mode, measure 24V_IN on F1 / J1's input pad.
steps.rationale: Confirms the wall supply actually reaches the board. 0 V here = adapter, cable or jack J1 at fault, not the board.

steps.instruction: Still powered. V DC mode, measure VIN at R49 (after the fuse).
steps.rationale: 24 V at J1 but 0 V here ⇒ F1 is open. This is the measurement that separates F1 from everything else.

steps.instruction: Powered. V DC mode, measure U14's LPC_VCC output (the 3V3 standby output pin).
steps.rationale: If VIN arrives fine but the board stays dead: 0 V here = U14 dead or a short on LPC_VCC. Separates the standby stage from the input.

steps.instruction: Summary: we target F1 (iron replacement), a short on VIN (component hunt with the thermal camera), or U14 depending on which point drops.
steps.rationale: Steers the next step toward the right repair based on the results.

input.note: Cold continuity across F1 — fuse intact, blown-fuse hypothesis ruled out.

input.content:
# F1 (input fuse) — continuity

- s_1 (cold, ohmmeter across F1 pads): **0.02 Ω → fuse INTACT**.
  → Blown-fuse hypothesis (was top suspect ~0.50) RULED OUT.
  → Shifts weight to: short on VIN that hasn't blown fuse, OR fault downstream (U14 standby / sequencing). Next: VIN→GND diode test (s_2).

frame.text:
✅ **F1 = 0.02 Ω → the fuse is good.** So we rule out suspect #1. That shifts the weight to two leads: either a **short on VIN** that hasn't (yet) blown the fuse, or a fault **downstream** (the U14 standby stage).

That's exactly what step 2 will settle.

**Step 2 — VIN→GND short check (board still off).** Multimeter in **diode mode** (the ▷| symbol), **black probe on a ground** (mounting screw, shield, or J1's GND leg), **red probe on the VIN side of `R49`**.

- **> 0.3 V** (or OL climbing) → no hard short, VIN is healthy → we'll go powered to see where it drops.
- **< 0.05 V** (near 0) → **hard short to ground** on VIN: that's what's keeping the board dead, and it must be located (thermal camera) before anything else.

Note me the diode-mode value.

steps.instruction: Board OFF (nothing connected). Multimeter in ohmmeter mode, one probe on each F1 pad.
steps.rationale: Tests the fuse continuity directly. OL = blown fuse, our suspect #1.

steps.instruction: Board OFF. Diode mode, black probe on GND, red probe on the VIN side of R49. Note the value.
steps.rationale: Detects a VIN→GND short BEFORE replacing F1: a short would blow a fresh fuse again. <0.05 V = hard short to chase.

steps.instruction: Plug in the 24 V adapter (batteries removed). V DC mode, measure 24V_IN on F1 / J1's input pad.
steps.rationale: Confirms the wall supply actually reaches the board. 0 V here = adapter, cable or jack J1 at fault, not the board.

steps.instruction: Still powered. V DC mode, measure VIN at R49 (after the fuse).
steps.rationale: 24 V at J1 but 0 V here ⇒ F1 is open. This is the measurement that separates F1 from everything else.

steps.instruction: Powered. V DC mode, measure U14's LPC_VCC output (the 3V3 standby output pin).
steps.rationale: If VIN arrives fine but the board stays dead: 0 V here = U14 dead or a short on LPC_VCC. Separates the standby stage from the input.

steps.instruction: Summary: we target F1 (iron replacement), a short on VIN (component hunt with the thermal camera), or U14 depending on which point drops.
steps.rationale: Steers the next step toward the right repair based on the results.

input.note: Diode-mode VIN→GND at R49, board OFF. 0.5V = healthy junction drop, NO short on VIN bus.

input.content:
# VIN bus — short test & voltage

- s_2 (cold, diode-mode VIN→GND at R49): **0.5 V → NO short** on VIN bus.
  Combined with F1 intact: input-bus hardware healthy at rail level.
  → Remaining hypotheses: (a) no input voltage actually arriving (J1/cable/adapter), (b) fault downstream in standby (U14 / LPC_VCC). Powered tests next (s_3 @ J1, s_4 @ R49).

frame.text:
✅ **VIN→GND = 0.5 V → no short.** The input bus is healthy hardware-wise (fuse good + no short). So we go **powered**.

**Step 3 — 24 V present at the input (board powered).** Plug in the 24 V adapter, **batteries removed**. Multimeter in **V DC**, black probe on GND, red probe on **F1's input pad / J1's center pin**.

- **~24 V** (9–32 V acceptable) → the wall supply reaches the board fine → the fault is on the board, we go down.
- **~0 V** → it's upstream: adapter, cable or jack `J1` (cold joint). In that case we won't look further on the board.

Quick safety reminder: if you smell or see anything heating while powered, cut it immediately. Give me the voltage read at the input.

steps.instruction: Board OFF (nothing connected). Multimeter in ohmmeter mode, one probe on each F1 pad.
steps.rationale: Tests the fuse continuity directly. OL = blown fuse, our suspect #1.

steps.instruction: Board OFF. Diode mode, black probe on GND, red probe on the VIN side of R49. Note the value.
steps.rationale: Detects a VIN→GND short BEFORE replacing F1: a short would blow a fresh fuse again. <0.05 V = hard short to chase.

steps.instruction: Plug in the 24 V adapter (batteries removed). V DC mode, measure 24V_IN on F1 / J1's input pad.
steps.rationale: Confirms the wall supply actually reaches the board. 0 V here = adapter, cable or jack J1 at fault, not the board.

steps.instruction: Still powered. V DC mode, measure VIN at R49 (after the fuse).
steps.rationale: 24 V at J1 but 0 V here ⇒ F1 is open. This is the measurement that separates F1 from everything else.

steps.instruction: Powered. V DC mode, measure U14's LPC_VCC output (the 3V3 standby output pin).
steps.rationale: If VIN arrives fine but the board stays dead: 0 V here = U14 dead or a short on LPC_VCC. Separates the standby stage from the input.

steps.instruction: Summary: we target F1 (iron replacement), a short on VIN (component hunt with the thermal camera), or U14 depending on which point drops.
steps.rationale: Steers the next step toward the right repair based on the results.

frame.text:
✅ **24 V at the input → the wall supply reaches the board fine.** So the problem is *on* the board, downstream of the input. We tighten.

**Step 4 — VIN after the fuse (still powered).** Multimeter in V DC, red probe on the **VIN side of `R49`** (same point as step 2, but live this time), black probe on GND.

- **~24 V** → the input is spotless up to the system bus; the fault is in the **standby** stage → we'll measure U14 (step 5).
- **~0 V** while you have 24 V at the input → something between the jack and R49 isn't passing (the inrush/charger path), we'll dig there.

Give me the voltage at R49.

input.note: Powered (24V adapter, batteries out), V DC at J1/F1 input pad. Wall power confirmed reaching board.

measurement.note: Powered (24V adapter, batteries out), V DC at J1/F1 input pad. Wall power confirmed reaching board.

frame.text: Waiting for your VIN reading at R49 for step 4.

steps.instruction: Board OFF (nothing connected). Multimeter in ohmmeter mode, one probe on each F1 pad.
steps.rationale: Tests the fuse continuity directly. OL = blown fuse, our suspect #1.

steps.instruction: Board OFF. Diode mode, black probe on GND, red probe on the VIN side of R49. Note the value.
steps.rationale: Detects a VIN→GND short BEFORE replacing F1: a short would blow a fresh fuse again. <0.05 V = hard short to chase.

steps.instruction: Plug in the 24 V adapter (batteries removed). V DC mode, measure 24V_IN on F1 / J1's input pad.
steps.rationale: Confirms the wall supply actually reaches the board. 0 V here = adapter, cable or jack J1 at fault, not the board.

steps.instruction: Still powered. V DC mode, measure VIN at R49 (after the fuse).
steps.rationale: 24 V at J1 but 0 V here ⇒ F1 is open. This is the measurement that separates F1 from everything else.

steps.instruction: Powered. V DC mode, measure U14's LPC_VCC output (the 3V3 standby output pin).
steps.rationale: If VIN arrives fine but the board stays dead: 0 V here = U14 dead or a short on LPC_VCC. Separates the standby stage from the input.

steps.instruction: Summary: we target F1 (iron replacement), a short on VIN (component hunt with the thermal camera), or U14 depending on which point drops.
steps.rationale: Steers the next step toward the right repair based on the results.

frame.text:
✅ **VIN = 24 V at R49.** Verdict on the input: **the entire input stage is healthy** — 24 V at the input, fuse good, no short, and the VIN bus is indeed at 24 V. So the fault is **downstream, in the standby generation**.

That shifts my main suspect: it's no longer F1, it's now **`U14`, the 3.3 V *always-on* regulator** (~0.55, rule `rule-lpc-vcc-dead-002`). It's the one that must permanently produce the **LPC_VCC** rail that wakes controller U18. If it's dead, the whole board stays off — exactly your symptom.

**Step 5 — U14's LPC_VCC output (powered).** `U14` is a small 6-pin package. In V DC, measure its **LPC_VCC output pin** (the 3.3 V rail side).

- **~3.3 V** → standby is alive, the problem is even further down (U18/sequencing side) — we'll reconnect.
- **~0 V** → **U14 is dead** (open output) or **LPC_VCC is shorted**. We'll tell them apart right after with a diode test on LPC_VCC, board off.

To aim well: shall I show you U14 and its surroundings?

input.note: Powered, V DC VIN at R49 (after fuse). Bus at 24V → entire input stage healthy. Fault is downstream in standby gen.

input.new_string:
## Plan / status
Guided protocol (6 steps) in progress. Results so far:
- s_1 F1 continuity = 0.02Ω → fuse GOOD. Blown-fuse hypothesis RULED OUT.
- s_2 VIN→GND diode (cold) = 0.5V → NO short on VIN.
- s_3 24V_IN @ J1 (powered) = 24V → wall power reaches board.
- s_4 VIN @ R49 (powered) = 24V → entire INPUT STAGE HEALTHY.
=> Fault is DOWNSTREAM in standby generation.

## REVISED top suspect
U14 (always-on 3V3 LDO → LPC_VCC) ~0.55, per rule-lpc-vcc-dead-002.
Next discriminator: s_5 = LPC_VCC voltage at U14 output. If 0V → U14 dead OR LPC_VCC short → then cold diode-mode LPC_VCC→GND to split U14-dead vs rail-short (isolate U18).
STATUS: awaiting s_5 (LPC_VCC @ U14).

## Plan / status
Proposed guided protocol (6 steps): cold F1 continuity → cold VIN→GND diode short test → powered 24V@J1 → powered VIN@R49 → powered LPC_VCC@U14 → ack/branch.
STATUS: protocol proposed, awaiting tech's first measurements.

measurement.note: Powered, V DC VIN at R49 (after fuse). Bus at 24V → entire input stage healthy. Fault is downstream in standby gen.

frame.text: Yes — I'll highlight it so you aim right: `U14` is a small 6-pin package. I'm also lighting up the whole **LPC_VCC** net: its output pin is the one that joins that net.

There — `U14` is zoomed and highlighted in orange, and the 20 points of the **LPC_VCC** net are lit: U14's output pin touching that net is where you measure.

Go ahead, give me the **LPC_VCC voltage at U14's output** (V DC, board powered).

steps.instruction: Board OFF (nothing connected). Multimeter in ohmmeter mode, one probe on each F1 pad.
steps.rationale: Tests the fuse continuity directly. OL = blown fuse, our suspect #1.

steps.instruction: Board OFF. Diode mode, black probe on GND, red probe on the VIN side of R49. Note the value.
steps.rationale: Detects a VIN→GND short BEFORE replacing F1: a short would blow a fresh fuse again. <0.05 V = hard short to chase.

steps.instruction: Plug in the 24 V adapter (batteries removed). V DC mode, measure 24V_IN on F1 / J1's input pad.
steps.rationale: Confirms the wall supply actually reaches the board. 0 V here = adapter, cable or jack J1 at fault, not the board.

steps.instruction: Still powered. V DC mode, measure VIN at R49 (after the fuse).
steps.rationale: 24 V at J1 but 0 V here ⇒ F1 is open. This is the measurement that separates F1 from everything else.

steps.instruction: Powered. V DC mode, measure U14's LPC_VCC output (the 3V3 standby output pin).
steps.rationale: If VIN arrives fine but the board stays dead: 0 V here = U14 dead or a short on LPC_VCC. Separates the standby stage from the input.

steps.instruction: Summary: we target F1 (iron replacement), a short on VIN (component hunt with the thermal camera), or U14 depending on which point drops.
steps.rationale: Steers the next step toward the right repair based on the results.

frame.text: ✅ **LPC_VCC = 3.3 V!** And that's an important **plot twist**. Let me validate the downstream chain before reinterpreting.

input.note: Powered, V DC LPC_VCC at U14 output. Standby rail ALIVE — U14 is fine, contradicts blown-standby hypothesis.

measurement.note: Powered, V DC LPC_VCC at U14 output. Standby rail ALIVE — U14 is fine, contradicts blown-standby hypothesis.

frame.text:
**Big twist, and it's good news.** Let's recap what the 5 measurements prove:

| Point | Reading | Verdict |
|---|---|---|
| F1 | 0.02 Ω | fuse OK |
| VIN→GND (cold) | 0.5 V | no short |
| 24V_IN @ J1 | 24 V | supply arrives |
| VIN @ R49 | 24 V | system bus OK |
| **LPC_VCC @ U14** | **3.3 V** | **standby ALIVE** |

So **power is not the problem**: the input *and* the *always-on* domain are perfectly healthy. `U14` does produce the 3.3 V that feeds `U18`, the system controller (LPC11U24).

What's happening: `U18` isn't starting the **main rails**. On the Reform, they do **not** come up automatically when you plug in — `U18` must receive a **power-on command** (power button / the `1P` command over UART). It's its `5V_PWR_EN` → `U7` (+5 V) and `3V3_PWR_EN` → `U12` (+3 ⟨?V3⟩) lines that wake the board. And a key detail: the `D11` LED is on the **+3V3 main** rail, not on LPC_VCC — so it stays **off in standby, which is normal**.

I'm closing the protocol and showing you the real chain to investigate:

input.reason: 5 mesures montrent entrée + standby 100% sains (24V_IN, F1 OK, VIN 24V, LPC_VCC 3.3V). La carte n'est pas morte côté alim : elle est en veille. Pivot vers la séquence d'allumage U18 → U7/U12.
input.verdict: Étage d'entrée et standby sains ; le défaut est dans la séquence d'allumage (U18 doit asserter 5V_PWR_EN/3V3_PWR_EN), pas dans l'alimentation.

annotations.label: U18 LPC - sequencer (has LPC_VCC=3V3, so powered)
LED on +3V3 main (off in standby = normal)

input.new_string:
## s_5 RESULT — PIVOT
LPC_VCC @ U14 = 3.3V → U14 FINE, standby ALIVE. U18 (LPC11U24) is powered.
=> Power tree (input + standby) is 100% healthy. Board is NOT dead — it's in STANDBY.
D11 sits on +3V3 MAIN (not LPC_VCC) → dark in standby is NORMAL, not a fault.

## NEW framing — power-on sequence
Main rails +5V (U7) / +3V3 (U12) only come up when U18 asserts 5V_PWR_EN / 3V3_PWR_EN,
triggered by a power-ON command (1P over LPC UART, or power button / keyboard circle key).
U18 nets incl: 5V_PWR_EN, 3V3_PWR_EN, 1V2/AUX/USB/PCIE_PWR_EN, LPC_UART2_TX/RX.

Next discriminator = issue power-ON and watch:
- UART responds + rails up on 1P  -> board OK, was standby (or D11 dead indicator).
- UART responds but rails collapse -> switched-rail short (rule-3v3-switched-short-003).
- No UART response at all          -> U18 not running (fw / reset / xtal / GPIO damage).

## OPEN QUESTIONS for tech
- Bare motherboard or full laptop? Keyboard attached?
- UART/serial access to LPC available?
- When "dead" reported: did they ever press power / send 1P? Did D11 ever flicker?
STATUS: protocol completed; awaiting tech answers before proposing power-on protocol.

steps.rationale: 24 V at J1 but 0 V here ⇒ F1 is open. This is the measurement that separates F1 from everything else.

steps.instruction: Powered. V DC mode, measure U14's LPC_VCC output (the 3V3 standby output pin).
steps.rationale: If VIN arrives fine but the board stays dead: 0 V here = U14 dead or a short on LPC_VCC. Separates the standby stage from the input.

steps.instruction: Summary: we target F1 (iron replacement), a short on VIN (component hunt with the thermal camera), or U14 depending on which point drops.
steps.rationale: Steers the next step toward the right repair based on the results.

history_tail.reason: 5 mesures montrent entrée + standby 100% sains (24V_IN, F1 OK, VIN 24V, LPC_VCC 3.3V). La carte n'est pas morte côté alim : elle est en veille. Pivot vers la séquence d'allumage U18 → U7/U12.
history_tail.verdict: Étage d'entrée et standby sains ; le défaut est dans la séquence d'allumage (U18 doit asserter 5V_PWR_EN/3V3_PWR_EN), pas dans l'alimentation.

frame.label: U18 LPC - sequencer (has LPC_VCC=3V3, so powered)
LED on +3V3 main (off in standby = normal)
